1: wayhî rāʿāḇ bāʾāreṣ miḷḷĕḇad hārāʿāḇ hārīʾšôn ʾăšer hāyāh bîmê ʾaḇrāhām — "Now there was a famine in the land, besides the earlier famine that was in the days of Abraham." The narrator's parenthetical comparison sets up the structural parallel: Abraham went to Egypt in a famine (Genesis 12:10); Isaac is told not to go (v2). The phrase miḷḷĕḇad (besides, apart from) explicitly distinguishes this famine from Abraham's, preventing the reader from conflating the episodes. The parallel invites comparison: Isaac's response to the same crisis will differ from his father's, and the differences are theologically instructive.
2: wayyērāʾ ʾēlāyw YHWH wayyōʾmer ʾal-tēreḏ miṣrāymāh — "YHWH appeared to him and said, 'Do not go down to Egypt.'" The theophany formula wayyērāʾ ʾēlāyw YHWH (YHWH appeared to him) — Niphal of rāʾāh — is the same form used for theophanies to Abraham (Genesis 12:7; Genesis 17:1; Genesis 18:1). Isaac's first recorded direct encounter with YHWH begins with the prohibition against the Egypt option. The command ʾal-tēreḏ miṣrāymāh (do not go down to Egypt) reverses the Abraham pattern deliberately — the covenant land is where Isaac is to remain, even in famine. The same directive will be given to Jacob with a different result (Genesis 46:3-4): Jacob is told to go down; Isaac is told to stay. The covenant's geographical particularity is being worked out differently in each generation.
3: gûr bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt wĕʾehyeh ʿimmĕḵā ûʾăḇārĕḵekā — "Sojourn in this land, and I will be with you and will bless you." The verb gûr (H1481, to sojourn, live as a resident alien) — Abraham used the same term for his own status in Canaan (Genesis 23:4, gēr). Isaac is told to sojourn, not settle permanently — he remains a temporary resident in the land of promise. The divine-presence formula ʾehyeh ʿimmĕḵā ("I will be with you") is the covenant presence formula that recurs throughout the patriarchal narratives (Genesis 28:15; Genesis 31:3; Genesis 48:21) and becomes foundational for the Exodus (ʾehyeh ʾăšer ʾehyeh, "I am who I am/I will be what I will be," Exodus 3:14).
4: wĕhirbêtî ʾet-zarʿăḵā kĕkoḵĕḇê haššāmayim wĕnātattî lĕzarʿăḵā ʾēt kol-hāʾărāṣōt hāʾēl wĕhitbārăḵû bĕzarʿăḵā kol gôyê hāʾāreṣ — "I will multiply your seed as the stars of the sky, and I will give to your seed all these lands, and through your seed all the nations of the earth shall bless themselves." The three-element promise to Abraham (Genesis 12:2-3; Genesis 15:5; Genesis 17:4-8) is now confirmed to Isaac: multiplication of seed (stars), land, and blessing to all nations. Zarʿăḵā (your seed) is the covenant-continuity term throughout Genesis — the singular collective noun that will carry the Abrahamic promise forward. The blessing formula wĕhitbārăḵû bĕzarʿăḵā uses the Hithpael of bāraḵ — reflexive: "shall bless themselves by your seed."
5: ʿēqeḇ ʾăšer-šāmaʿ ʾaḇrāhām bĕqōlî wayyišmōr mišmartî miṣwōtay ḥuqqôtay wĕtôrōtāy — "because Abraham obeyed my voice and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws." Four terms accumulate for Abraham's obedience: mišmeret (charge, watch-keeping duty), miṣwāh (commandment, explicit directive), ḥuqqāh (statute, engraved ordinance), and tôrāh (instruction, law). The same tetrad appears in Leviticus 18:30; 22:9 for Israel's covenant obligations. This is remarkable: Abraham is credited with keeping the Mosaic-sounding categories of covenant law before Sinai — not because the Mosaic law existed yet, but because the covenant relationship with YHWH has always involved obedient hearing (šāmaʿ bĕqôlî, to hear/obey my voice). The grounding of Isaac's blessing in Abraham's past obedience shows that covenant blessing is cumulative and transmittable.
6: wayyēšeḇ yiṣḥāq bigrār — "So Isaac settled in Gerar." The bare compliance statement after the theophany and extensive covenant promise is a narrative device signaling immediate, simple obedience. Isaac stays where he is told to stay; his obedience is uncontested at this stage of the narrative. Gerar is the Philistine territory where Abraham also sojourned with Abimelech (Genesis 20:1-18) — the geographic repetition reinforces the structural parallel between the Abraham and Isaac episodes in this chapter. The same place, the same king (Abimelech — likely a dynastic title rather than the same individual), the same deception, and the same resolution.
7: wayyišʾălû ʾanĕšê hammāqôm lĕʾišĕtô wayyōʾmer ʾăḥōtî hîʾ kî yārēʾ lēʾmōr ʾišĕtî — "The men of the place asked him about his wife. He said, 'She is my sister,' for he feared to say, 'She is my wife.'" The wife-sister deception occurs three times in Genesis (Genesis 12:10-20 for Abraham in Egypt; Genesis 20:1-18 for Abraham with Abimelech; here for Isaac with the same Abimelech). The repetition is structurally deliberate: each patriarch faces the same temptation (protect yourself by endangering your wife) and each chooses the same deception. Yārēʾ (feared) — Isaac's motivation is stated explicitly: fear. The fear is legitimate (beautiful wives were dangerous assets), but the solution — deception — compromises the covenant wife's safety for the husband's benefit.
8: wayyaṣṣēp ʾăḇîmeleḵ melek pĕlištîm bĕʿaḏ haḥallôn wayyarʾ wĕhinnēh yiṣḥāq mĕṣaḥēq ʾēt riqāh ʾišĕtô — "Abimelech king of the Philistines looked out a window and saw — and behold — Isaac laughing/sporting with Rebekah his wife." The key word is mĕṣaḥēq — Piel participle of ṣāḥaq (to laugh, play, sport, caress) — the same root as Isaac's own name (yiṣḥāq = "he laughs"). The text makes the wordplay unavoidable: yiṣḥāq was seen mĕṣaḥēq — "Isaac was Isaacing." The Piel of ṣāḥaq has a range from laughing to playing to sexual intimacy (cf. Genesis 39:14,17 where Potiphar's wife uses it to accuse Joseph). The context of a husband and wife makes the sexual nuance primary — Abimelech concludes they are married from this intimate behavior, not from overhearing a conversation.
9: ʾāḵēn hinnēh ʾišĕtĕḵā hîʾ wĕʾêk ʾāmartā ʾăḥōtî hîʾ — "She is clearly your wife! How did you say, 'She is my sister'?" The adverb ʾāḵēn (surely, indeed, clearly) marks Abimelech's conclusion as certain — what he saw was unambiguous evidence of marriage. The king's challenge is direct and legitimate: Isaac's deception endangered the king's household by the potential of one of his men taking Isaac's wife — which would constitute adultery (the same concern Abimelech expressed to Abraham in Genesis 20:3-6). Isaac's response, "I thought I might die on account of her" (pen-ʾāmût ʿālehā), acknowledges the fear motivation without defending the deception.
10: mah-zōʾt ʿāśîtā llānû kĕmĕʿaṭ šāḵaḇ ʾaḥaḏ hāʿām ʾet-ʾišĕtĕḵā wĕhēḇêʾtā ʿālênû ʾāšām — "What is this you have done to us? One of the people might easily have lain with your wife, and you would have brought guilt upon us." ʾĀšām (H817, guilt, guilt-offering, offense requiring restitution) — in Levitical law the ʾāšām is the guilt-offering required when one has wronged another party and must make restitution (Leviticus 5:14-6:7). Abimelech's use of the term shows his awareness that lying with another man's wife is a legally accountable wrong. The king of the Philistines is applying the principle that YHWH applied to him in Genesis 20:6 — the same covenantal moral framework governs pagan as well as covenant interactions.
11: wayyĕṣaw ʾăḇîmeleḵ ʾet-kol-hāʿām lēʾmōr hannōgēaʿ bāʾîš hazzeh ûḇĕʾišĕtô môt yûmāt — "Abimelech commanded all the people: 'Whoever touches this man or his wife shall certainly be put to death.'" The death formula môt yûmāt (infinitive absolute + imperfect = "dying he shall die," emphatic death penalty) — the same formula used in the capital case laws of the Torah (Exodus 21:12-17; Leviticus 20:2,9-16). Abimelech's protection order over Isaac and Rebekah is more emphatic than the ordinary protection a guest might expect. It reflects both his personal moral concern (expressed in v10) and the practical recognition that YHWH is with Isaac (which he will state explicitly in v28).
12: wayyizraʿ yiṣḥāq bāʾāreṣ hahîʾ wayyimṣāʾ baššānāh hahîʾ mēʾāh šĕʿārîm wayyĕḇāreḵēhû YHWH — "Isaac sowed in that land and reaped in that year a hundredfold, and YHWH blessed him." Mēʾāh šĕʿārîm (a hundred gates/measures) — the šaʿar here is a grain measure, not a city gate: "a hundredfold." In the ancient world a 10:1 return on seed was considered outstanding; 100:1 is miraculous. The causal wayyĕḇāreḵēhû YHWH (YHWH blessed him) — the narrator attributes the abundance directly to covenant blessing, not to Isaac's agricultural skill. The abundance in the famine year is a sign of YHWH's presence with the covenant heir in the land he commanded him to stay in.
13: wayyigdal hāʾîš wayyēlek hālôḵ wĕgāḏēl ʿaḏ kî-gāḏal mĕʾōḏ — "The man became great, and kept growing greater until he became very great." The Qal perfect gāḏal + the idiom hālôḵ wĕgāḏēl (going and growing) — the infinitive absolute of hālaḵ combined with the Qal participle of gāḏal creates the picture of continuous, ongoing growth. The same construction appears in 2 Samuel 5:10 for David: "David grew and grew" (hālôḵ wĕgāḏôl). The threefold use of gāḏal in this verse (became great / growing greater / very great) is emphatic accumulation — the narrator insists on the magnitude of Isaac's prosperity to set up the Philistine envy in v14.
14: wayhî-lô miqnēh-ṣōʾn ûmiqnēh-ḇāqār waʿăḇuddāh rabbāh wayyĕqannĕʾû ʾōtô pĕlištîm — "He had flocks and herds and a great household, and the Philistines envied him." Qānnāʾ (Piel of qānāʾ, H7065, to be jealous/envious) — a strong word; the same root describes God's "jealousy" (ʾēl qannāʾ, Exodus 20:5; Genesis 34:14) and the jealousy that drove Joseph's brothers to sell him (Acts 7:9). The Philistines' envy is the socio-political pressure that generates the expulsion of v16 and the well-disputes of vv19-21. Covenant blessing in the land of the foreigner creates tension — a pattern that recurs from Joseph in Egypt to Israel in Egypt to Daniel in Babylon.
15: wĕkol-habbĕʾērōt ʾăšer ḥāpĕrû ʿaḇdê ʾāḇîw bîmê ʾaḇrāhām ʾāḇîw sittĕmûm pĕlištîm wayyĕmalĕʾûm ʿāpār — "Now all the wells that his father's servants had dug in the days of his father Abraham, the Philistines stopped them up and filled them with dirt." The destruction of wells in the ancient Near East was an act of war and a deliberate denial of future habitation rights — wells represented permanent water-claim in a semi-arid landscape. The Philistines' actions against Abraham's wells are an erasure of the patriarchal covenant presence in the land. Isaac's response (v18) — reopening the same wells and giving them the same names — is an act of covenant reaffirmation: reclaiming what was his father's in the covenant territory.
16: ʿāṣamt mimmennû mĕʾōḏ — "you have become too powerful for us." The verb ʿāṣam (H6105, to be numerous, strong, mighty) — the same verb used for Israel's multiplication in Egypt (Exodus 1:7,20) and for YHWH's mighty arm (Deuteronomy 9:29; Genesis 26:8). The Philistines' fear of Isaac's growing power parallels Pharaoh's fear of Israel's growth (Exodus 1:9-10: "the people of Israel are more numerous and stronger than we"). Both situations involve the covenant people's prosperity creating host-nation anxiety that leads to expulsion or oppression. The pattern of blessing → growth → host-fear → expulsion is a recurring structure in the covenant narrative.
17: wayyēlek miššām yiṣḥāq wayyaḥan bĕnaḥal gĕrār wayyēšeḇ šām — "So Isaac moved away from there and camped in the Valley of Gerar and settled there." The verbs sequence — moved (hālaḵ), camped (ḥānāh), settled (yāšaḇ) — follow the patriarchal movement pattern of Abraham (Genesis 12:9; Genesis 13:18; Genesis 21:34). Isaac's movement into the valley is a compromise: he leaves the city but stays in the region, pressing the question of how far he must go before he finds undisputed space. The Valley of Gerar (naḥal gĕrār) is a wadi/watercourse — the kind of terrain where digging wells would be productive, setting up the disputes of vv19-21.
18: wayyāšāb yiṣḥāq wayyaḥpōr ʾet-bĕʾĕrōt hammayim ʾăšer ḥāpĕrû bîmê ʾaḇrāhām ʾāḇîw — "Isaac dug again the wells of water that had been dug in the days of his father Abraham." Ḥāpar (H2658, to dig, bore into) — the verb used for digging wells throughout this chapter (vv18-22,25,32). Isaac's reversion to Abraham's wells is a legal and covenantal claim: these are not new wells but the restoration of Abraham's property rights. Wayyiqrāʾ lāhen šēmôt kĕšēmōt ʾăšer-qārāʾ lāhen ʾāḇîw — "he gave them the same names that his father had given them" — the renaming is continuity of ownership claim. Abraham's names mark the history; Isaac's repetition of those names asserts the covenant inheritance is unbroken even through the Philistine erasure.
19: wayyeḥpĕrû ʿaḇdê-yiṣḥāq bannāḥal wayyimṣĕʾû šām bĕʾēr mayim ḥayyîm — "Isaac's servants dug in the valley and found there a well of living/fresh water." Mayim ḥayyîm (living water, flowing spring water) — ḥāy (H2416, alive, living) — the phrase distinguishes spring-fed, flowing water from cistern water (stored rainwater). Living water was the highest quality — pure, fresh, reliable — and appears throughout OT legislation (Leviticus 14:5,50; Genesis 15:13; Numbers 19:17) and wisdom/prophetic literature (Jeremiah 2:13; Genesis 17:13; Zechariah 14:8). Jesus's use of "living water" in John 4:10-14 and 7:38 is charged with this OT background — the new covenant blessing is described in the language of the freshest, purest, most life-sustaining water.
20: wayyĕrîḇû rōʿê gĕrār ʿim-rōʿê yiṣḥāq lēʾmōr lānû hāmāyim wayyiqrāʾ šēm-habbĕʾēr ʿēśeq kî hitʿassĕqû ʿimmô — "The herdsmen of Gerar quarreled with Isaac's herdsmen, saying, 'The water is ours!' So he named the well Esek, because they disputed with him." ʿĒśeq (H6219, contention, dispute) — from the root ʿāsaq (to dispute, press a claim). The naming convention in vv20-22 is an etiology: each well's name records what happened there. Isaac's response to the dispute is to move on (wayyelek miššām, v21) — he does not fight for the well. This patience under repeated opposition is Isaac's characteristic response throughout the chapter, culminating in the Rehoboth statement of v22.
21: wayyaḥpĕrû bĕʾēr ʾaḥeret wayyĕrîḇû gam-ʿāleyhā wayyiqrāʾ šĕmāh śiṭnāh — "They dug another well, and they quarreled over that one also, and he named it Sitnah." Śiṭnāh (H7855, opposition, hostility, accusation) — from the root śāṭan (H7853, to oppose, to be an adversary), the root that gives the title "Satan" (the Adversary). The naming of a well "Adversary/Accusation" reflects the experience of enmity that was purely hostile and unjust — not a legitimate competing claim but pure opposition. The progression Esek (contention) → Sitnah (adversarial hostility) shows escalation. Isaac again moves without fighting back.
22: wayyaʿtēq miššām wayyaḥpōr bĕʾēr ʾaḥeret wĕlōʾ rāḇû ʿāleyhā wayyiqrāʾ šĕmāh rĕḥōḇôt wayyōʾmer kî-ʿattāh hirḥîḇ YHWH lānû ûpārînû bāʾāreṣ — "He moved from there and dug another well, and they did not quarrel over it. He named it Rehoboth, saying, 'For now YHWH has made room for us, and we will be fruitful in the land.'" Rĕḥōḇôt (H7344, wide places, spacious areas) — from rāḥaḇ (H7337, to be wide, to make room). Isaac interprets the cessation of conflict as divine provision of space — hirḥîḇ YHWH lānû ("YHWH has made room for us"). The movement from Esek to Sitnah to Rehoboth is a theological narrative of patient endurance yielding to divine provision: God does not give the space at first but creates it through the progression of trials. ûpārînû bāʾāreṣ — "we will be fruitful in the land" — pārāh (H6509, to be fruitful) echoes the creation blessing (Genesis 1:28) and the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 17:6) — covenant fruitfulness finally has room to express itself.
23: wayyaʿal miššām bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ — "From there he went up to Beersheba." The movement wayyaʿal (went up) — the directional opposite of yāraḏ (to go down) — moves from the lowland Valley of Gerar back toward the highland of Beersheba, the location of the patriarchal covenant with Abimelech (Genesis 21:22-34) and the altar of Abraham (Genesis 21:33). The journey to Beersheba is a movement toward the sacred site of covenant history, and the theophany of v24 confirms this — YHWH appears at the place of covenant establishment.
24: ʾānōḵî ʾĕlōhê ʾaḇrāhām ʾāḇîḵā ʾal-tîrāʾ kî-ʾittĕḵā ʾānōḵî ûḇēraḵtîḵā wĕhirḇêtî ʾet-zarʿăḵā baʿăḇûr ʾaḇrāhām ʿaḇdî — "I am the God of your father Abraham. Do not be afraid, for I am with you; I will bless you and multiply your seed, for the sake of Abraham my servant." The self-identification formula ʾānōḵî ʾĕlōhê ʾāḇîḵā — "I am the God of your father" — is the covenantal self-introduction (cf. Genesis 28:13; Genesis 31:42; Genesis 32:9; Exodus 3:6). The fear formula ʾal-tîrāʾ (do not be afraid) accompanies every major patriarchal covenant encounter (Genesis 15:1; Genesis 21:17; Genesis 26:24; Genesis 28:13; Genesis 46:3). Baʿăḇûr ʾaḇrāhām ʿaḇdî — "for the sake of Abraham my servant" — the covenant benefits to Isaac are explicitly grounded in Abraham's prior covenant status. The title ʿeḇeḏ (servant) for Abraham is the highest honorific in the OT covenant vocabulary (also used for Moses, Joshua, David).
25: wayyiḇen šām mizḇēaḥ wayyiqrāʾ bĕšēm YHWH wayyeṭ-šām ʾohŏlô wayyikrû šām ʿaḇdê-yiṣḥāq bĕʾēr — "He built an altar there and called on the name of YHWH, and he pitched his tent there, and Isaac's servants dug a well there." The three-fold covenant response — altar, calling on YHWH's name, tent — mirrors the Abraham pattern exactly: Abraham built altars at Shechem (Genesis 12:7), Bethel (Genesis 12:8 — also "called on the name"), Mamre (Genesis 13:18), and Beersheba (Genesis 21:33). Isaac's replication of these covenant markers at Beersheba is a conscious continuation of the patriarchal covenant practice. The altar (mizḇēaḥ) is the formal site of encounter; calling on the name (qāraʾ bĕšēm YHWH) is the covenant worship formula; the tent marks covenant residence; the well marks covenant provision.
26: waʾăḇîmeleḵ hālaḵ ʾēlāyw migĕrār waʾăḥuzzat mērēʿēhû ûpîḵōl śar-ṣĕḇāʾô — "Abimelech came to him from Gerar with Ahuzzath his adviser and Phicol the commander of his forces." The same Abimelech-Phicol pair appeared in the Abraham covenant (Genesis 21:22,32). If these are the same individuals, 80+ years have passed — more likely "Abimelech" is a Philistine dynastic title (like "Pharaoh" for Egypt) and "Phicol" similarly. Mēraʿēhû — "his friend/companion" (from rēaʿ, H7453, friend, associate, companion) — Ahuzzath is Abimelech's personal advisor, the king's inner council.
27: lāmmāh bāʾtem ʾēlay wĕʾattem śĕnēʾtem ʾōtî wattĕšalĕḥûnî mēʾittĕkem — "Why have you come to me, since you hated me and sent me away from you?" Isaac's challenge uses śānēʾ (H8130, to hate, to be hostile toward) — a strong term for covenant enmity. Isaac's memory of the Philistines' hostility (v15 — stopping his father's wells; v16 — expelling him) grounds his suspicion. The question is not rude but honest — Isaac names the prior hostility before engaging with the covenant request. This directness is not typical of ANE diplomatic discourse, which would normally open with politeness; Isaac's frankness shows both his awareness of the history and his willingness to negotiate from a position of truth.
28: rāʾôh rāʾînû kî-hāyāh YHWH ʿimmāḵ — "We have plainly seen that YHWH was with you." The emphatic construction rāʾôh rāʾînû (Qal infinitive absolute + perfect = "seeing we have seen") — the doubled form intensifies: their perception is certain, observed, undeniable. The basis for Abimelech's treaty request is the observation of divine favor: YHWH's presence with Isaac is so evident that even the Philistine king can name it. This pattern — covenant people's prosperity making YHWH's presence observable to Gentile rulers — recurs in Joseph (Genesis 39:3), Moses (Exodus 18:9-11), and the missional theology of Isaiah 2:2-4 and Genesis 60:1-3. The Gentile's recognition of YHWH's covenantal presence is the basis for covenant diplomacy.
29: ʾim-taʿăśeh ʿimmānû rāʿāh kaʾăšer lōʾ nĕgaʿnûḵā wĕkaʾăšer ʿāśînû ʿimmĕḵā raq-ṭôḇ wattĕšallĕḥăḵā bĕšālôm wĕʾattāh ʿattāh bĕrûḵ YHWH — "that you will not do us harm, just as we have not touched you and have done to you nothing but good and sent you away in peace. You are now the blessed of the LORD." The Philistines' self-description of their conduct is selective (Isaac experienced their stopping his wells and expelling him), but the diplomatic formula focuses on the present — they are appealing for treaty on the basis of not having physically harmed Isaac. ʿAttāh bĕrûḵ YHWH — "you are blessed of YHWH" — the Philistine king applies the covenant title to Isaac; the blessing Isaac carries is so visible that his adversary names it. The phrase will return in the Boaz-Ruth narrative (Ruth 3:10) and the Psalms (Psalms 115:15; Genesis 128:4).
30: wayyaʿaś lāhem mišteh wayyōʾkĕlû wayyišĕtû — "He made them a feast and they ate and drank." The covenant meal (mišteh, H4960, feast/drinking occasion) seals the treaty: in ANE covenant practice, eating together was the ratification of an agreement — the shared table bound the parties morally and socially. Compare Abraham's covenant meal with Abimelech (Genesis 21:27-31) and Jacob's covenant meal with Laban (Genesis 31:54). The pattern of covenant ratification through shared eating connects this episode to the broader covenant tradition and anticipates the covenant-meal theology of Exodus 24:11 and the Lord's Supper.
31: wayyaškimû babbōqer wayyiššāḇĕʿû ʾîš lĕʾāḥîw wayyĕšallĕḥēm yiṣḥāq wayyēlĕḵû mēʾittô bĕšālôm — "Early in the morning they swore an oath to one another. Then Isaac sent them off, and they went away from him in peace." Wayyaškimû (they rose early) — early rising for the covenant oath ratification parallels Abraham's early rising for covenant acts (Genesis 22:3: the Moriah journey; Genesis 21:14: sending away Hagar). The oath (šĕḇûʿāh) formally sealed the covenant begun by the meal of v30. Bĕšālôm (in peace, in wholeness) — the word šālôm (H7965) here means covenant-peace, the wholeness of relations between two parties who have formalized mutual non-aggression. The Philistines leave in šālôm — the same word Isaac's servants will use for Beersheba (Beer-sheba = seven/oath).
32: wayyōʾmĕrû lô māṣāʾnû māyim — "They said to him, 'We have found water!'" The discovery of the new well on the same day as the covenant with Abimelech is a narrative juxtaposition — covenant with man and covenant provision from God are simultaneous. The servants' report uses māṣāʾ (H4672, to find, to come upon) — the discovery of water is framed as finding something given rather than digging something out; the providence is credited to discovery, not to labor. The timing (same day as the treaty) invites the reader to see YHWH's provision as confirming the covenant settlement.
33: wayyiqrāʾ ʾōtāh šiḇʿāh ʿal-kēn šēm-hāʿîr bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh — "He called it Shibah; therefore the name of the city has been Beersheba to this day." The place name bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ carries a double etymology: (1) from šeḇa (H7651, seven) — Abraham's covenant with Abimelech involved seven lambs as a witness (Genesis 21:28-31); (2) from šāḇaʿ (H7650, to swear) — both the Abraham covenant (Genesis 21:31) and the Isaac covenant (v31 here) involve oaths. The name encodes both "well of seven" and "well of the oath" — both etymologies are simultaneously valid, reflecting the double oath (Abraham's and Isaac's) that attach to the location. ʿAḏ hayyôm hazzeh ("to this day") is the narrator's confirmation that the name has persisted to his own time.
34: wayhî ʿēśāw ben-ʾarbāʿîm šānāh wayyiqqaḥ ʾiššāh ʾet-yĕhûḏît bat-bĕʾērî haḥittî wĕʾet-bāśmat bat-ʾêlôn haḥittî — "When Esau was forty years old, he took as wife Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite." Esau's marriages at age 40 mirror Isaac's marriage age (v20). But the contrast is sharp: Isaac married within the Abrahamic family (Rebekah from Paddan Aram); Esau marries Hittite women — Canaanites, outside the covenant family. The specific identification "Hittites" (ḥittî) echoes the Ephron-Hittite transaction of Genesis 23 — the non-covenant people from whom Abraham bought the burial cave. Esau's Hittite wives represent the same pattern as the birthright sale: trading the covenant for immediate local advantage.
35: wattihyênāh môrat rûaḥ lĕyiṣḥāq ûlĕriqāh — "and they were a source of bitterness of spirit to Isaac and Rebekah." The idiom môrat rûaḥ (bitterness of spirit) — mārar (H4843, to be bitter) + rûaḥ (H7307, spirit/breath) — a grief that sits in the breath, in the inner life. The same idiom appears in 1 Samuel 1:10 for Hannah's anguish. The narrative coda to Genesis 26 connects directly to the motivation for Rebekah's schemes in Genesis 27: if Esau's Hittite wives already cause Isaac and Rebekah such distress, Rebekah's actions to prevent the covenant blessing from going to Esau become more comprehensible — she fears Jacob will marry Hittite women too (Genesis 27:46; Genesis 28:1-2), and the covenant line will be completely absorbed into the Canaanite population.
